commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. First impression of a new video game?  (5)(6)
2. You can't cut through it  (3)(2)(3)
3. Shelters some look to when duty calls?  (3)(6)
4. Establishments where smoking is allowed  (3)(6)
5. Takes off-road?  (7)


START BUTTON
CUL DE SAC
TAX DODGES
RIB JOINTS
RECALLS
I loved this!. (Though I confess that my first try for "Game point" was Antler, which made me chuckle, but after realizing the answer is ARCADE I had to retract the chuckle.)
Either I'm getting way faster or the puzzles are getting easier. I solved Wed. and Thursday in my typical Mon / Tuesday range.

A pleasant and very gentle Thursday puzzle from Joe Marquez. 
Wednesday seemed Tuesdayish; I think this would be a fine Wednesday. I guess we have wait a bit longer for PUREEVIL.
Cute. Clever. Wednesday. Like popcorn.
Cute idea, but I think the fills are kind of ugly, tbh.

I liked the puzzle, but was it Thursday level? My average is around 20 minutes, yet today's puzzle was over in a new PB of 6'25". 

Meanwhile, the US DST change means we get the puzzles an hour earlier in AEDT (1pm, day of), and another hour earlier when we finish DST in a few weeks (12pm), so I guess I'll have to take my lunch break earlier. :)
Loved it! I knew the game was afoot at '2nd largest city', then going clue by clue, wait for it.... ha! Very satisfying.

My better angels must have been looking out for me tonight, as I caught on to the FALLENANGEL trick fairly quickly.  Not as challenging as I had hoped, but fun nevertheless.  Any puzzle that includes Doctor STRANGELOVE is ok in my book.

I learned about ANGELADAVIS back in my college days through the music of Ralph McTell.  There’s a reference to her in his song Zimmerman Blues.  All these years later the song and she helped me out with the puzzle.

Ive been deep in the archive lately, doing ThFSs from early 1994, and let me tell you....anyone whining about how easy one of these late week puzzles is, go back and do some from the 90s....you will be humbled.
This feels like one of those crosswords that was more fun to construct than it actually is to solve.

Cute but I wanted more than three, and also was disappointed that only TAO of the three was a legit word (perhaps there is someone among the 8 billion on this earth named BRAINA -- if there's Briana, why not Braina? -- or the surname LOSAES ...). I mean, surely Evangeline has a place in this puzzle. Surely Wrangel Island! Surely we can find a column on which to place Fra Angelico and his compatriot Michelangelo. But I do like seeing Angela Davis. Talk about an afro. Talk about body. What a great human being. Link to an image of her beautiful head below. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angela_Davis#/media/File:Angela_Davis,_1974.jpg
Very fun until I couldn't figure out what I did wrong. Seriously, NOT allowing the rebus? Spent half my time trying to find where I erred. Grrr.
Please just give us crosswords. This kind of thing is awful, no fun at all, and trying too hard to be clever. I want to do a crossword puzzle. That's why I'm here.
Fastest Thursday ever for me, in about one-third my average time. I enjoyed the puzzle, but it should have been on a Tuesday IMO.

Bring back HARDCORE Thursdays.

We need a ROAST in the comments. I nominate Matty boi. @Matt

Bodø/Glimt, ENCORE! 

Whole LOTTA Red hits different.

Woot woot.... My Thursday PB. Felt like a Monday... Is it me?? :)
Have a good day everyone.
Dr. Strangelove had a strange glove, a "strangle glove!"
fallen angel but you keep the "a"? didn't really make sense to me. more like fallen ngel.

Meh, not for me. I love a good rebus if I’m remotely expecting it, but I usually cant stand these sorts of trailing letter themes and today was no exception.  Especially when the actual answer to the clue isn’t even a word. 

Also the clueing seemed a bit easy for a Thursday, but I appreciate that it’s tough to tune the difficulty of a puzzle.

Love Dr. Strangelove though.

Pluto is no longer a planet, Chicago is no longer No.2 city in the US... Things I've learned growing up is no longer true and I have no idea who Playboi Carti is.
Upon filling 26D, yet another relic of my youth came to the forefront and it is now an earworm. Is it just me or did anyone experience that tune by Alphaville of the same title begin to play in your head?

So this was a tight clever theme. One could argue about its reliance on proper nouns and whether somehow the fill should create an existing word or phrase (probably impossible in this case, and neither  of those really bothered me). And the rest of the fill/clues might have been a little easy in terms of challenge but pretty good in my opinion. I’m going to be very specific in my critique and again it’s on the editors:
Why hand this theme to us on a silver platter?
I was pretty well stumped in the NW and feeling, great, I’ve got a puzzle on my hands. And then:
Mad scientist in a 1964 Kubrick film?
Second most populous US City?
Are you kidding me? Two gimmes and the theme is ruined?
Why not just say Fictional 60s mad scientist, at least, and some clue for LA that isn’t immediately obvious?
Top it off, the revealer. Not that it wasn’t obvious already, but if it weren’t,  “heavenly outcast” is pretty much a direct definition. Maybe clue it just “Lucifer, for example?” Or something remotely less straightforward?
I’m sorry, just those three changes would have elevated this puzzle immensely for me and I just don’t understand why.
On another note anyone else wonder if a DORAPHOBE is afraid of FUN? ‘Cause Dora the Explorer is pretty fun.
@Sam. “Largo” doesn’t that have something to do with a key?
SO SAD about those  'FALLEN ANGELS ', which I learned about in my Catholic elementary school days.

Well, that was something in a non-rebus Thursday kind of way. Sometimes luck and head-scratching only take you so far . . .

Rescued that gold star after flyspecking  and landing on PUREEVIL - as ANGELADAVIS didn't come to mind until the alphabet run solved the mystery of PUREERIL.

Isn't that a laundry detergent, or a hand sanitizer?

Boy howdy, you'd have to wash my mouth out with some Purell -  sussing out where one hinky letter was hiding.

It's Thursday eve, folks.  The world ain't perfect, but it's what we got - try to smile and keep one foot in front of the other.  Happy Solving, all.
FYI, there’s a spelling error in a clue: “Curiousity” (Midi, 12 down)

"They can be found “falling” in the crossing Down entries"

Way less of these, please!

Finished 30 minutes faster than my usual 
Thursday. Delighted!

My forecast for the commentary surrounding this (highly accessible) puzzle?

Cloudy, with a strong chance of _But if only LOSAES and BRAINA were actual words!_

Ciao for now.
Great fun? I noticed the celestial being reasonably early, which gave me a happy solve.

Very easy.  Another somewhat disappointing Thursday, if still a bit better.  The bones of the theme were almost immediately apparent and the fill was, let's say, not challenging.  The follow-it-down-and-resume thing was interesting though (even as it left nonsense in the grid).

XWstats says "Easy" at present.

I wonder if this is a victim of editorial policy or whether these clues were presented as-is.  In any case I think I liked yesterday's a lot better...

Theme wasn't my favorite but wasn't too hard considering the answer was obviously LOS ANGELES.

I had the most trouble in the northwest and southwest.  In southwest I thought forever the answer to the locale for trivia was BAR.  Once I finally got PUB it all fell into place.

Northwest, it took figuring out MAHLER whose name I am familiar with but know nothing about.  I felt DENOTE, ALIT, RARER, AMBLE were all awkward enough answers and I hadn't heard the term LENTO since piano lessons decades ago that it was just hard to get a foothold in that region.

Average cluing today made for a couple frustrating areas but no real satisfying payoffs.
I have a problem with the Mini, 2D. For probably 70 years now, IPA/I.P.A. has been International Phonetic Alphabet. What in the world is PALE?

Got this one in just over half an hour. Might have been quicker but had entered 'angel' into the boxes where an 'a' was sufficient. 

Still, pretty easy. Strange that just a few months ago Thursday was out of reach for me. Does it seem like the last few weeks have been soft?

Sorry. I’m spoiled I know. 
But that was just too common a Thursday trick. 
And if the puzzle is going be based on something like that, then you gotta ratchet up the clues. 

I mean “second most populous city in the US”.  That’s a Monday-level clue. But in this case, it just gives away the trick immediately.  

On the editors. Not the constructor. 
It might have been a Thursday grid.  But the clueing turned it into a Monday. 

It would have next level to have the themed across entries be real words.  TAO. Nice. 
BRAINA. Close
LOSAES. yuk. 

Anyway. I appreciate the puzzle for what it is.

Geez. Sub-14 minutes on a Thursday? Of course, any puzzle with “Das Lied von der Erde” is aces with me, but that absurd time (for me) included entering Boston for the second largest city! I sort of figured out the trick in retrospect, but I got the music before understanding, and wasn’t satisfied with the solution I saw. Maybe I missed something? After checking, is a TANGELA a thing? Tangelo, yes, but since when does close count in a crossword? And I see how ANGEL falls three times, but then you curve around in an unspecified way to finish the entry? I was really surprised, btw, that Boston is that big. But even more surprised that such a boneheaded guess on a Thursday could resolve so quickly because of easy crosses.
A lot of quibbles from people here. I just want to say I loved it. Well done Joe.

I made this far more difficult than I needed to. I caught on quickly (or so I thought) and entered ANGEL as a rebus in the across squares where the A belonged. I didn’t get to the revealer until going through the downs and my rebus entries weren’t making sense. I tried ANGEL/A up until i noticed that ANGEL was also spelled out in the downs. Then I got to the revealer 🤦🏼‍♀️ then erased each one and changed to an A 😆 Nice enough theme. Must have been on the same wavelength as Joe because I was looking for Maya Angelou in the grid 😉 Thanks, Joe!

When I started the puzzle in the NW corner (where else) I was geared up for a Thursday, rife with rebuses, so failed to spot the easy fills. Dropped down and all of a sudden I was writing away.  

After the puzzle was done, I kept looking for more Los Angeles(es) or something, and finally got it that the theme was just the three downward ANGELs.  So this time, simplicity was a trap and I was the one to FALL for it.

Thanks, Joe.  Dr. Strangelove is one of my all time favorite Peter Sellars' roles, and I start grinning every time I think of him.  

(Not so funny now, with the drone factor.)

I enjoyed it but this is the easiest Thursday I've seen in a while. Was feeling real proud of myself for a minute, catching some of the usual tricks until I realized it was just an easier puzzle. A win is a win though and the point is to have fun.

This one was alright. Got confused a little by the TAO comment in the article (thought the answer wasn’t, in fact, TAO) but I did enjoy the trick, while I did feel it was pretty standard and easy to pick up on. I’ve been going through Shortz’s 101 favorite puzzles lately though, so perhaps I’ve been spoiled in the past couple weeks. I give it a 7/10 for a Thursday overall!
Oh, I’m a little sad, I first thought “Game point?” solved to ANTLER. Ha!

I recall seeing "Dr. Strangelove" at the movies when it came out.  I have no idea how or why this happened, because I rarely got to go to any movies, let alone movies as infamous as "Dr. Strangelove".  

Maybe the adults were parking us for a couple of hours.

Anyway, at the age of 11/12 the movie was a total mystery to me.  The first farcical satire I'd ever seen, but I didn't even have a category for it.  

But it was about my most fundamental fear at the time:  nuclear war. 

As the years went by I came to love the brilliance of the movie, one of Kubrik's finest.  Then again, my opinion of "his finest" only lasts until I think of another of his.
OK my first time that it did not take the rebus entry for music. Had to change back to As. Otherwise fun. Despite it, fun.
Feels like this week's crosswords are all one day ahead of schedule.
So, my Monday and Wednesday times were half my average times, and Tuesday and Thursday were five minutes better than my average times. That means Friday and Saturday will be diabolical! 😞
I figured out the trick almost immediately and breezed through the rest. I would have broken my Thursday record if I hadn't entered ANGEL instead of A. I don't get it. BRAINA et al make no sense.
My fastest Thursday yet and I only got the theme two-thirds of the way through! And here I was dreading that I had missed a rebus.
Gentlemen! You can't fight in here! This is the War Room!
[Lead single from "Music for the Masses"] STRANGELOVE https://youtu.be/aTKuoyyYqAM?si=D7Ydpe9RqW0CKad_
Would anyone mind throwing me a bone in the bottom right hand corner? Thx!
This is odd. When I was still getting “horsefeathers!” while finding no errors, I checked the puzzle - all was well, it just marked all the rebuses wrong. Anyone else?
Fun but too easy for a Thursday.

Had me going for a while. Thought this must be a rebus puzzle and did that and no happy music. The ANGELS are fallen instead. Clever. Agree this was a pretty easy Thursday but i enjoyed it. Fun cluing.  Well done

Well I filled in the grid but couldn’t get the music, went to the comments and took the rebuses out but still no music. Gave up and revealed. My downfall was Tucci. I had exalts, instead of exults, and since I didn’t know that name had no way to see my error. Bummer. Now I’m going to go look up those two words.
A line I love from Dr. STRANGELOVE: "Gentlemen!  You can't fight in here!  This is a war room!"

@Al in Pittsburgh (or is Cairo, NY?) gave us a little, but how 'bouts a little more Milton?: Who first seduc’d them to that fowl revolt? Th’ infernal Serpent; he it was, whose guile Stird up with Envy and Revenge, deceiv’d The Mother of Mankinde, what time his Pride Had cast him out from Heav’n, with all his Host Of Rebel Angels, by whose aid aspiring To set himself in Glory above his Peers, He trusted to have equal’d the most High, If he oppos’d; and with ambitious aim Against the Throne and Monarchy of God Rais’d impious War in Heav’n and Battel proud With vain attempt. Him the Almighty Power Hurld headlong flaming from th’ Ethereal Skie With hideous ruine and combustion down To bottomless perdition, there to dwell In Adamantine Chains and penal Fire, Who durst defie th’ Omnipotent to Arms. Nine times the Space that measures Day and Night To mortal men, he with his horrid crew Lay vanquisht, rowling in the fiery Gulfe Confounded though immortal . . . Bill and Gustave saw the whole thing: https://tinyurl.com/ym8ysbty https://tinyurl.com/3a452545 *** MAHLER's *Das Lied von der Erde* is a "symphonic song-cycle," but they're Lieder, not ARIAs; there's a slow movement, but it's marked "Langsam," not LENTO; the orchestration includes percussion, but no SNARE DRUMS; finally, the style is Austrian post-romanticism, not SKA. So close. *** As the Two-Headed Man, sometimes I think with my Brain B, at other times with my . . .

Speaking of movies, there's one I really like called "CHANGING LANES" starring Ben Affleck and Samuel Jackson. From 2002, it's about two people in conflict who are desperate to resolve their differences. An emotional Everest. https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0264472/

Seems like the puzzle should have been considered solved whether you entered the ANGEL rebus, or just the A. Took a while for me to first check all my answers, then try replacing with A’s. But still, clever puzzle. Enjoyed it.

Well, I love the crossword mind that came across FALLEN ANGEL and thought “Crossword theme!”

I love the theme answers in which the ANGEL doesn't come from the word "angel" -- CHANGE LANES, TANGELO, and STRANGELOVE. These are hard to find.  There’s “evangelist”, which post-puzzle exploring tells me is not an outgrowth of “angel”.  What else?

I also liked the musical references in the box – MAHLER, LENTO, SNARES, ARIA – not to mention COUNT (which triggers “Basie” in my mind) and column five, which contains two answers that can be wrenched to have musical meanings – DE NOTE, and A TONE.

Finally, I loved [Be important], a non-tricky clue that perfectly describes its answer, yet it stymied me but good until the crosses revealed COUNT, upon which I was washed over by a huge aha.  Great moment for me.

Much feel-good from your puzzle, Joe.  Thank you for making it!
I put my ANGELs in as rebuses and that was evidently not correct. Took me forever to figure out how I had gone wrong! Ugh! Great puzzle otherwise.

If you’re really going to “get down to business” maybe you have to DIVEIN, and can’t stay ‘divine’? Discuss.

(Look, up in the sky! It’s a drone! It’s a UFO! It’s a formerly lofty puzzle franchise falling to earth as lightning!)

Interesting theme, and happily there were no circles to indicate where the three FALLENANGELs were located. Would have been especially nice if each of the three horizontals that made use of a "fallen angel" became a word in its own right (as TAO did, but not BRAINA or LOSAES). But that might well have been impossible to pull off.

Immediately entered OMAN (incorrectly) for "Mideast's Gulf of ___", so it was enjoyable to be able to enter OMAN (correctly) lower in the grid.

Having one of the angels "hidden" in ANGELA was a bit disappointing. Would have been nicer to have that "angel" better hidden, as the others were in CHANGELANES and STRANGELOVE.

All in all, a fun puzzle, but I wonder if the theme could have been handled a bit better. (Not by me!)

[By the way, the delay between finishing the puzzle and receiving confirmation of success that I reported earlier in the week seems to be gone, at least for me. So perhaps that bug has been squashed.]
Tuesday.
I think tues took me longer than thurs this week.

I apologize in advance
if all my PELE fan-girling
posts all get posted later.

Emus hate me today.

There was a clip with a montage.

It was cool.

Whatever.
I don't even care.

It's fine.

I'm *definitely* not grouchy.
A poor puzzle that was not fun to solve. Pass.
Who is BRAINA?? I was 17 in 2000, I’d think I’d remember a celebrity power couple by that name.

Clever construction, could've used tougher cluing, but fun. I once knew an Angela Davis, but not the Angela Davis. Went down a rabbit hole with turpitude. I've never thought of it as pure evil. Always heard of 'moral turpitude', but did not know that as a legal concept, moral turpitude is not so much about sexual/social morals but is more concerned with honesty, responsibility, and duty in business/legal/civic matters. The distinctions get rather arcane and arbitrary. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moral_turpitude Turpitude of all types has been too much in the news of late.

Tao? Citrus fruit portmantou? 
I don't get it?

If not for a clumsy finger in a couple of places, I likely would have had a Thursday PB…still, I finished around 60% under my average. I know some are complaining about it being easy but I enjoyed it and had to work for a few answers. I am a fan of the theme, which required me to think a bit outside the lines, so from one Joe to another: thank you to the constructor.

This was a fun puzzle and I solved it, but I was temporarily confused when the iPhone app version didn’t credit filling in the word “found three times in this puzzle” as a rebus. It wasn’t until I replaced those with the initial letter that I got the “solved” screen. 

Was this an app flaw or is the Times getting away from rebuses?

Anyway, I don’t mean to complain, it was a nice puzzle. Just curious. 

Thanks for any thoughts!
How to do a rebus puzzle without doing a rebus. Clever!
Fantastic puzzle!!! Only got caught up trying to enter a rebus answer when i didnt even need to. I loved it. :))))

The hard part for me was seeing that the ANGEL was a pure rebus in the Acrosses and written out in the Downs.  Once I saw that, though, everything got much easier. 
 
I should have thought of FALLEN ANGEL immediately, but it didn't occur to me until I saw the revealer clue.  Very cute!  The theme answers are beautifully embedded and I had fun with this one.

My "drawing" of the rebus ANGEL was very perfunctory.  No real body to speak of, much less wings.  A round filled-in dark circle for the head and a stick for the body.  Sort of looks like...is that a half-note?  Never mind: It's a prompt for me and it's more than good enough.

Re BRANGELINA: Is the portmanteau name of a celebrity couple one of the great inventions of recent times?  Just imagine all the previous generations that didn't have these to savor.  SO SAD.  Let me coin a few belatedly:
BOGALL
LAWRIAN
ELEANIN
TREPBURN
RICHBETH
LENONO

You're welcome.

I liked this puzzle fine.  It could have been better/crunchier, but it was a clever idea, reasonably well executed.

It strikes me as amusing, however, to see people complain about rebuses *not* being accepted.  I guess, in addition to the rebus haters, we have people who want to see rebuses where none exist.
There are no rebuses here.  The ANGELS are FALLEN.  
It might be amusing to come up with "boxed" or "square" ANGELS, but that's a puzzle for another Thursday.

As for those who are wondering how LOSAES and BRAINA are words, please read the column before coming to the comments.

Typical Thursday workout for me. Was appropriately puzzled for quite a while but STRANGELOVE was a gimme and then it wasn't hard for LOSANGELES to dawn on me. A couple of the others were still a bit of workout - BRANGELINA being completely unfamiliar to me - but managed to work it all out. One puzzle find today - a Tuesday from May 21, 2002 by Paula Gamache. The 'reveal' in that one was a pair of clues/answers: 48a -"With 25 across, quote hidden in 20, 37 and 53 across" And those two answers were: JULIUS CAESAR And those referenced answers (and I'm highlighting the key in the first one - you can figure out the other two): deathinVENIce DIVIDINGINTWO INTHEVICINITY Here's that link; https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/21/2002&g=20&d=A ....

Interesting solve – once I saw I wasn't going to fit more than one letter in a cel, scooted around the grid clockwise and ended up at the NW. A coffee run happened. Upon return got the AHA to understand 11A (among others) and hit the clock at my second fastest Thursday time. Thanks for the fun! Sub Wednesday time, full on Thursday head-slap. Not a bad combo!
Was just thinking this felt like more of a Thursday than a Wednesday, only to complete it and discover it is, in fact, Thursday! Whew

Today’s poem made from words found in today’s puzzle

portrait of a supercouple at rest

a/ picture the world in a big cup
slowly… slowly…
d/ step up
a/ drink!
Solved in under 15 minutes today. Either I'm getting really good at xwords or this was an easy Thursday. I hope the former. Some days, everything just clicks.
Any idea what happened to the midi? It links to a "page not available".

Enjoyed it

Think it was a little on the easier side
Had to look up a few references, but otherwise a pretty breezy Thursday. Ended up one minute shy of my best.
Slip ons are not sandals.

Nice. 
I realised something was going on with ANGEL, although a rebus didn't work. 
I didn't know about ANGELA DAVIS and prison reform, didn't know GTO or LOTTA, thought TAO might be a type of American TAngerine Orange! So that area held me back until I looked up/revealed some.

A really satisfying solve - I like when the revealer really opens up the puzzle for me. This one had to marinate for a little while. Also one of the first Thursdays that came without lookups, we're making progress!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

commentBody: Rather angelic for a Thursday...
I was stuck for a while thinking the whole “Angel” thing was a clunky rebus situation. Otherwise pretty fun

"Braina?" "Losaes?"
Another crossword that drifts too far for my taste into the realm of "the gimmick is more important than the puzzle's integrity."
I get the gag. It's not that compelling a gag, but it's fine --- IF the resulting words mean anything. I cast my gaze upon my completed puzzle and ask "What are these absurdities and how did we all get here?" 
It's a tragedy from which I may not recover,and it only seems to be a trend that is growing. I weep for the children, for tomorrow and tomorrow's solvers may face grids with no meaningful words at all, yet a really solid gimmick. This will lead to the breakdown of civil society and the erosion of table manners. 
Can we not just eliminate some of these overly gimmicky newfd puzzles? 
Now you're thinking "An error was made, surely 'newfd' is not a word." Ah, but you're forgetting the gimmick. 
See how it feels??? Ha.
Am I the only one bothered by the fact that the revealer feels like it should've (could've?) followed the pattern but didn't?

The most exciting 10 minutes for me every week: Wednesdays 9:50 PM till 10 PM, right before the Thursday puzzle hits. Over the years, I have seen some really doozy Thursday puzzles, and anticipation that this next one will be as amazing is my eternal hope. Judged from that perspective, today’s puzzle was about 85°C. Please take this with a huge grain of salt, because this author has never been successful in creating a puzzle himself. Though he’s tried.:)

My first drive-by comment:

Happy 7D wasn't Wawa.
All drums are double-headed.

It's funny how I mentioned the invention of Gore Tex yesterday, and it turned up as an answer on Jeopardy! last night, in the context of a serendipitous discovery. I know the show is taped weeks in advance, but what the actual?

Fine Thursday gimmick, STRANGELOVE was one of my first fills, so no problem linking it with LOS ANGELES. "Mudslide or hurricane" for DRINK was amusing. Also, a prairie fire is a shot of tequila and Tabasco sauce.
An exquisite puzzle

Oooo new UI in the app today. I like it. 
I was very proud of myself that I guessed the trick immediately upon seeing tangelo, and confirmed with Los Angeles next door. Usually I'm at least 2/3 through before I get the Thursday trick! Solid theme and fun puzzle, if a little quick. And I loved all the classical music clues!
This was a fun puzzle to solve, thank you!

A nice Thursday. A tad easier than some of the rebus offerings. Needed the revealer to get the theme; I could see there was something going on with the nonsense entries, but it was a great Aha moment to spot the ANGELS.

Referring back to last Sunday’s puzzle; in our monthly village pub quiz last night the date of this years International Women’s Day was one of the questions. Yes! Fist 👊 Still didn’t win us the game though, not on our best form last night. I blame the storm raging outside and knowing we were going to have to brave it to get home.

Very fun puzzle! For once I got the theme in time for it to help me solve. That northwest corner was really tough for me. Started with Wagner and switched to Rahner thinking "that's a composer, right?" No: theologian. Not the first time seminary brain has taken over...last night when writing about TV I wrote "last semester" instead of "last season." Anyway, this puzzle had me at ANGELADAVIS. I'm literally always saying read "Abolition. Feminism. Now."!

This was a brilliant puzzle, so I don't think I'll read any other comments today (busy day in any case, handbell practice and so on). Sometimes when I really love a puzzle and have struggled just enough to find a solution to what is going on in the constructor's mind . . . well, it just a bit unsettling to see complaints. And somehow there are always a few who just hate a given puzzle. 
I'm so grateful to Joe Marquez, and to the whole team. I love Sam's comments as well.
Thanks you guys :-) Off for my daily walk now in the glorious sunshine and cool weather.

The column’s explanations that “filling in the squares only lets us write BRAINA”  and the “available squares only give us LOSAES” have the solution backwards.  You end up with those nonsensical replies only after you’ve figured out the trick.

In my case I began by scanning the clues for a revealer and FALLEN ANGEL was my first fill, so I knew what was coming.  Otherwise anyone trying to fill those answers would be unable to proceed until they got the corresponding down clues.

Really liked this puzzle, one of the first Thursdays I’ve completed. POSSE got me good. Also I have never heard of TANGELO but the hint that it was a portmanteau meant I could eventually get it anyway. I had to dig deep into the recesses of my brain to find BRANGELINA, having figured out that was the only spot for the final ANGEL to appear.

The next level of this is when you leave the rebus out as required and both crossing fills remain actual words themselves, even if they seem nonsensical to the clue.

"Tao" does this, but the other two do not. 

There have been puzzles that got to that level in the past.
Very elegant. After finishing, I indeed went to a dictionary to find the connection between TAO and citrus! 😞. Well done!

It may be possible to arrive at the comments section without scrolling through the Wordplay column; if so, I don't know how.  But every day, so many commenters seems to have accomplished this.

Slow down and read it, folks. The columns often ask and answer the same questions that get (mis)posted here, and besides, it's usually a fun read.
After all the kerfuffle about TNT earlier this week, I'm surprised nobody has objected to it being clued similarly today. (25A)
Very cute puzzle today.
Interesting that it was not a rebus puzzle.  That's why I was not getting a completed puzzle notice.  Finally had to go check out RexWordPuzzle to see where I was making a mistake.

As a child living just a few miles away from the Marin Civic Center I will never associate Angela Davis as anything other than the buyer of multiple firearms resulted in the deaths of multiple people including a judge taken hostage that had a sawed off shotgun taped to his neck.

And... a late puzzle find. Can't remember if I ever linked this one before. But anyway - A Sunday from October 17, 2010 by Patrick Blindauer with the title "Figure of speech." Don't need to mention the clues - here are some of the answers: CONW8WITTY W8UNTILDARK THEL8L8SHOW P8ONPLACE B8ANDSWITCH SK8BOARDER SUBW8RAIN Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/17/2010&g=25&d=A ...
Doraphobia isn't the fear of gifts?
Today’s crossword was o e of the worst I have seen.
How is this not a rebus puzzle... Smh

Where is ‘Losaes”? 

Is it a misspelling of “Los Angeles”? 

When I got to that one, I just stopped. The puzzle maker is trying too hard to be cute.

This was a fun puzzle and quite clever idea. I think the revealer clue isn't too obscure, and once I realized how it worked, there are only so many places it could go. Tricks like this are well-implemented when at least one of the affected answers has a clear, definite answer that doesn't fit, so you know trickery is afoot.

However, I think what's throwing people off is that usually (though not always) clues that are modified by a gimmick like this still read as actual words or phrases, but here we're left with BRAINA and LOSAES (at least TAO is a real term), which can yield second-guessing.

A surprisingly fast solve for me for a Thursday. I thought it might be a rebus, but realized quickly that the crosses wouldn’t work. I had LOSAES, filled in and then when I got STRANGELOVE and the revealer I was able to figure out the rest without too much trouble other than not realizing I had FALLEANGELS when I needed FALLENANGEL. Once I found that mistake the SE corner succumbed. 

I liked it overall. Enough “easy” clues to help with the crosses a bit of confusion about the theme to keep it interesting.

Way off topic, but with current events I couldn't help but think of a parody of an old Beach Boys song, which was a girl's name. 

And... that song title was actually an answer in two puzzles. Just curious to see if anyone will figure that out. 

...
gimme a break Brangelina and Angela Davis puzzle's vibe is no fun for anyone puzzles are suppose to be fun this one is not fun at ALL

Feeling a little out of sorts here... I didn't have a chance to visit the comments today until now, after I did Connections. There are no spoilers here, but I was surprised about something in that game, so I went and looked at their comments and I thought how strange it was that their comment section looks different from ours. 

And then I came here and now our comment section is different. I really liked having the search box, but now it seems that was short-lived or am I missing something? Is it hidden?

Today was go go go at work, and I was happy to finally be able to pop in here only to feel like I fell into some weird alternate universe. 

I'd be happy to search and see if anyone else is commented on this yet If I knew where the box went.

Like many I was confident this would be a rebus but I liked the mechanic. Feels like a long time since the last rebus, no?

Anyway, a good puzzle should make you feel clever when you inevitably solve it. This was a little on the easy side (10:01) but the “aha moment” was good.
I don't know if it's been mentioned, but thank you NYT for fixing the delay in getting the solved notification and ditty.
If they'd mentioned she was also a terrorist I'd have gotten Angela Davis right away.

What does citrus portmanteau mean?
Does it have something to do with Brangelina? Tangelo?
OK, yes, tangelo is a thing. Grown right nearby in the Rio Grande Valley (facepalm)
Why are we still using “Mideast”?

Today's XWP couldn't have been more made-to-order for me without having Leapfingerprints all over it.

A. I was in NewHaven back in the day of Wm Sloan Coffin when Doonesbury was just a newbie strip in the Yalie Dailey, and ANGELA_DAVIS came to town with her yard-wide AFRO. O MAN, and Huey Newton, also

B. I'll rewatch DR_STRANGELOVE every time I POSSE-bly can, it never gets old. How tickled I was when I realized the bombardier was James Earl Jones in an early role, recognized just by That Voice!

C. Followed Peter Sellers since the Gong Show, even unto The World of Henry ORIENT, where he's stalked by two tweens who find him SEXY although he's TUCCI-zee for words.

D. Wonderfully inclusive to find the Buddha GOTANA consorting among all the FALLEN_ANGELS.

There were plenty of places to scrabble but the only place I *really* gave my BRAIN A SIESTA was where I had DVORAK precede MAHLER, just because 6 letters fit, ach du lieber!

Y'all are cherubs for allowing me space to unload, especially since UNO how I tend to rAMBLE.

Nicely done, Joe Marquez!!

I stared at BRAINA for a good couple of minutes before I twigged what was going on.

This was particularly dopey because I’d done most of the puzzle early this morning before work and only had the full top left to go (except CHANGE… and ERA). I came back tonight to finish but struggled for a few minutes because I didn’t know any of the trivia, what a black opal is etc. 
Anyway, got them in the end. Googled BRAINA, nothing useful. Took longer than it should have, but I eventually remembered the trick, which I had gotten this morning but completely forgot about when I came back to it. This is not the first time I’ve done this on Thursday or Sunday when there’s a bug gap. Sometimes I read the column after finishing and I’ve no idea what the columnist is talking about until I go back and look a the bits I did early in the morning. These puzzles just do not seem to sink into my brain.

(This is a repost because attempt 1 doesn’t seem to have made it through the censors).

I don't think that was easy, but I was definitely on the constructors wavelength for this one, finishing in just under 11 min, which is closer to my Tuesday average time. As soon as I saw BRANGELINA the theme came quick. Would have love to see more FALLEN ANGELs, but I understand that there are grid constraints.

I FRICKING loved this puzzle!!!! Mainly, because for me, the difficulty was there, and early on in the puzzle to me, this seemed like it was going to be unsolvable (without lookups) until the theme clicked. Then, I was able to get the three (themed) down answers which helped me fill the rest.

These are themes I like. Themes, that while being difficult; actually help you solve the puzzle. Not just some obscure, arbitrary thought in a constructor’s head, that has no bearing on the rest of the puzzle…

Bravo!!! 

My favorite puzzle of the year so far.
Disappointed in yet another puzzle as easy as a Monday.    Zero fun, zero challenge in NYT crosswords for about the last year

@Lewis

In Crossword, labs are almost always dogs.

After thought re "turpitude": I first learned of this word in high school in news reports about some teacher somewhere who was being investigated for "moral turpitude". Being found liable for this would have allowed the district to fire him.

So I assumed all my life that the word meant "malfeasance" (which if in the "moral" category probably referred to something sexual). I had never known it to mean PURE EVIL. So ... now looking it up in real time ... apparently it ***doesn't*** mean exactly "pure" evil. It just means "evil", or "depravity", or "corruption". The "pure" qualifier is, I suppose, fine for a crossword. But the "pure" sense is not inherent in the meaning of turpitude.

@Dan

Thanks for checking.  It didn't sound right to me either.

@Dan

“Antler” was my first guess, too!

@Dan
Ditto for me on antler, and I was so proud of myself!

@Dan it does seem to be beyond standard evil, some kind of qualifier fits the definition better. 

Turpitude comes from Latin turpis, meaning "vile" or "base." The word is often heard in the phrase "moral turpitude," an expression used in law to designate an act or behavior that gravely violates the sentiment or accepted standard of the community. A criminal offense that involves moral turpitude is considered wrong or evil by moral standards, in addition to being the violation of a statute.
@Dan Porky’s featured the phrase “moral turpitude”.
@Barry Ancona Agreed.  Seemed like a Wednesday and my time (slow as I am comparatively to you and others here) reflected this.

Not an Angela Davis fan!  One August day in 1970 my husband and I showed up at the Marin California County Courthouse to get our marriage license.  The parking lot was full of police, etc.   Friends of Angela Davis were there shooting up a courtroom trying to free one of their friends who was on trial.  They killed a DA and paralyzed the judge.
@Teresa Same! Quite disappointed when I saw I had in fact solved the puzzle but it counted the rebus as mistakes. Otherwise, this would have been my first four day streak...
Same here! 😫

@Teresa

What rebus?  Seriously, what are you guys talking about?

@Teresa
Same. Finally gave up and broke my streak.
@B Some people (like me), put in the entire fallen "word" as a rebus instead of just an "a". Usually this is accepted in these cases, but not today. It cost us about a minute on our time.
@Teresa If it were a rebus (multiple letter entry), it would not work on the down clues. You need to notice that and conclude that it can’t be a rebus. It’s part of the puzzle.

@Gavin
There have been other puzzles with the exact same gimmick that took the rebus. Where they want the rebus in only the horizontal or vertical direction, but only the first letter in the other direction.
@Harold this isn’t one of those though. If the “angel” were in one square it wouldn’t qualify as the “fallen angel” that the revealer indicates.

@B
They thought there was a rebus, but it wasn’t there.
The fact that this is one of the most upvoted comments (though it’s still a bit early in the AM) is absolutely wild 🤣

@Harold

For instance?

@Katie

Well said, if perhaps not as tactfully as you might have.

Part of the problem, I think, is that, when there are rebuses, the app accepts just the first letter, because people complained about not knowing how (or not being able) to enter rebuses.
Those answers are equally wrong, but now some people seem to think that they are equivalent.

@Anonymous

Not in the least bit surprising.  This whole thread shows two trends recently:

1. Thanks to an agressive Games Only subscription blitz, there's a slew of new solvers who don't know all the tricks yet, and

2. A genre of comment where the poster blames the puzzle for their solving shortcomings.  "Seriously, not allowing the rebus?"  There was no rebus; no reason for it being a rebus.

Note:  I originally rebused "angel" into the blanks, but didn't yet have the crosses.  Once I saw the revealer, I got the downs and also realized it wasn't a rebus, and definitively so.

"There have been other puzzles with the exact same gimmick that took the rebus."  No, there haven't.  Show me just one that did.

Note:  Revealers such as FALLEN ANGEL or JLO (J low) from the other day indicate that there's something going on in the Downs.
@Gavin fallen angel sounds like the down clue is missing the whole word angel, so you're left with just the a
@Anonymous I know
@Teresa  I literally spent half my time trying to make sense of this comment. Then I spent the other half of my time trying to figure out how it became the most recommended comment.

@Candice

Thursday (and sometimes other days) tricks are here to stay.  You have a decision to make .

@Lewis
Hate to be the bearer of bad news, but, if "evangelist" isn't a daughter of "angel," it's certainly a first cousin: both come from the Greek verb "angello," which means "to bring a message." "Angel"=messenger (of God), "evangelist"=one who brings *good* ("ev-"=good) news (of the *Gospel*--which in turn, also means "good news.") In fact, I doubt that you could find a single word with the string A-N-G-E-L which doesn't derive ultimately from the Greek root, save the portmanteau "tangelo."

commentBody:
@Becky

I like the image of a feather-rumpled fallen angel riding bareback on a terrified emu, crop in one hand and trumpet in the other.

@Becky

it's a long-standing tradtion here in Crosslandia to refer to the comments moderation system as the emus. So named because "emu" is common crosswordese.

Asher B.,

Right angle, wrong ‘g’ sound and spelling.
And I say that as an actual Newf.
:)

@Jeff

Yes. I think a lot of revealers stand by themselves alone.

@Jerry How about these bongos? Are they double-headed? (from Wikipedia): "Bongos (Spanish: bongó) are an Afro-Cuban percussion instrument consisting of a pair of small open bottomed hand drums of different sizes." At least, that's what I thought of. I'm not an expert. But I did play the bass drum and the whole percussion section in the band in school one term! https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bongo_drum
@Jerry, actually, some drums are single-headed: frame drums, tambourines, timbales, toms, timpani, congas, bongos, tablas, and others.
@Jerry - So wrong, yet stated with such conviction.
